pi_items.source: task.json and setup_task.sh explicitly specify this filename as the expected output
task.json description specifies catenary: f(x) = 630 - 128*(cosh(x/128) - 1); verified against Gateway Arch mathematical models from multiple sources including Wikipedia and educational materials
task.json description specifies parabola: g(x) = 630*(1 - (x/315)^2); verified against standard parabolic approximation of Gateway Arch
Unicode representation of x^2; valid alternative representation in GeoGebra
St. Louis Gateway Arch official dimensions: 630 feet tall and 630 feet wide. Confirmed by Wikipedia, National Park Service, and multiple educational sources
Derived from Gateway Arch total width of 630 feet (centered coordinate system: -315 to +315). Confirmed in parabolic approximations where arch touches ground at x = ±315

privileged_info_summary: The St. Louis Gateway Arch is 630 feet high and 630 feet wide at its base. The catenary function f(x) = 630 - 128*(cosh(x/128) - 1) is a simplified educational approximation of the actual arch (official formula: y = 693.8597 - 68.7672 cosh(0.0100333 x)). The parabolic approximation g(x) = 630*(1 - (x/315)^2) is equivalent to y = -0.00635x² + 630 in standard form. Both mathematical models accurately represent the arch dimensions with height 630 and half-width 315. The catenary model uses 'cosh' (hyperbolic cosine), and the parabola uses x^2 or x² notation. The task requires creating a GeoGebra file named 'gateway_arch_comparison.ggb' that displays both functions.
